sections.heading: This Regulation lays down harmonised rules, inter alia, on:
sections.title: GDPR evaluation report by Commission
sections.summary: The Commission must evaluate GDPR by September 2028 and report findings to the European Parliament, Council, and Economic and Social Committee. The evaluation covers data access, use, economic impact, data sharing, and SME compliance, among other areas.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires a comprehensive evaluation of GDPR's impact on data sharing and processing, which informs future data transfer compliance frameworks.

requirements.text: By 12 September 2028, the Commission shall carry out an evaluation of this Regulation
The Commission shall submit a report on its main findings to the European Parliament and to the Council
The Commission shall submit a report to the European Economic and Social Committee
The evaluation shall assess situations of exceptional need for Article 15 and Chapter V implementation
The evaluation shall assess the impact of this Regulation on data innovation and monetisation
The evaluation shall assess the accessibility and use of different categories and types of data
The evaluation shall assess the exclusion of certain categories of enterprises as beneficiaries under Article 5
The evaluation shall assess the impact on intellectual property rights
The evaluation shall assess the impact on trade secrets
The evaluation shall assess the impact of the mechanism allowing data holders to refuse user requests
The evaluation shall assess the impact on SMEs regarding innovation and data processing services

sections.source_quote: By 12 September 2028, the Commission shall carry out an evaluation of this Regulation and submit a report on its main findings to the European Parliament and to the Council, and to the European Economic and Social Committee.

sections.title: GDPR evaluation report for SMEs
sections.summary: The Commission must evaluate GDPR by September 2028 and report findings to the European Parliament, Council, and Economic and Social Committee. The evaluation focuses on pricing and data processing services for SME providers within the Union.
sections.dtia_summary: Specifically assesses the impact of GDPR on SME data processing services, which affects data transfer compliance for small businesses.

requirements.text: By 12 September 2028, the Commission shall carry out an evaluation of this Regulation
The Commission shall submit a report on its main findings to the European Parliament and to the Council
The Commission shall submit a report to the European Economic and Social Committee
The evaluation shall assess pricing and diversity of data processing services offered within the Union
The evaluation shall focus on SME providers

sections.source_quote: By 12 September 2028, the Commission shall carry out an evaluation of this Regulation and submit a report on its main findings to the European Parliament and to the Council, and to the European Economic and Social Committee. That evaluation shall assess the impact of Articles 23 to 31 and Articles 34 and 35, in particular regarding pricing and the diversity of data processing services offered within the Union, with a special focus on SME providers.

sections.heading: This Regulation applies to:
sections.title: Penalty criteria for GDPR violations
sections.summary: Member States must consider EDIB recommendations when determining penalties for GDPR breaches. Penalties are calculated based on infringement severity, mitigation efforts, prior violations, financial impact, and other contextual factors. This applies to national authorities enforcing GDPR compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines penalty criteria for GDPR violations, which affects how data transfers are assessed for compliance with regulatory penalties.

requirements.text: Member States shall take into account the recommendations of the EDIB
Member States shall consider the following non-exhaustive criteria for penalty imposition: the nature, gravity, scale and duration of the infringement
Member States shall consider the following non-exhaustive criteria for penalty imposition: any action taken by the infringing party to mitigate or remedy the damage
Member States shall consider the following non-exhaustive criteria for penalty imposition: any previous infringements by the infringing party
Member States shall consider the following non-exhaustive criteria for penalty imposition: the financial benefits gained or losses avoided by the infringing party due to the infringement
Member States shall consider the following non-exhaustive criteria for penalty imposition: any other aggravating or mitigating factor applicable to the circumstances of the case
Member States shall consider the following non-exhaustive criteria for penalty imposition: the infringing party's annual turnover in the preceding financial year in the Union

sections.source_quote: Member States shall take into account the recommendations of the EDIB and the following non-exhaustive criteria for the imposition of penalties for infringements of this Regulation: (a) the nature, gravity, scale and duration of the infringement; (b) any action taken by the infringing party to mitigate or remedy the damage caused by the infringement; (c) any previous infringements by the infringing party; (d) the financial benefits gained or losses avoided by the infringing party due to the infringement, insofar as such benefits or losses can be reliably established; (e) any other aggravating or mitigating factor applicable to the circumstances of the case; (f) the infringing party's annual turnover in the preceding financial year in the Union.

sections.title: GDPR enforcement fines for Chapter V violations
sections.summary: The European Data Protection Supervisor may impose administrative fines for violations of Chapter V obligations up to the amount specified in Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. This applies to the European Data Protection Supervisor acting within its competence for GDPR enforcement.
sections.dtia_summary: Authorises fines for GDPR violations, which impacts data transfer compliance assessments by defining potential financial penalties for non-compliance.

requirements.text: For infringements of the obligations laid down in Chapter V of this Regulation, the European Data Protection Supervisor may impose administrative fines

sections.title: Data coordinator facilitates cooperation with authorities
sections.summary: The data coordinator, if designated, must act as a single point of contact for GDPR-related issues, ensure online public availability of data requests for public sector bodies, and inform the Commission annually about refusals to data sharing requests.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines the role of the data coordinator, which is relevant for DTIA when assessing coordination mechanisms between public and private entities for data sharing.

requirements.text: Data coordinator shall act as single point of contact for GDPR-related issues

sections.source_quote: The data coordinator, where such competent authority has been designated, shall: (a) act as the single point of contact for all issues related to the application of this Regulation; (b) ensure the online public availability of requests to make data available made by public sector bodies in the case of exceptional need under Chapter V and promote voluntary data sharing agreements between public sector bodies and data holders; (c) inform the Commission, on an annual basis, of the refusals notified under Article 4(2) and (8) and Article 5(11).

sections.title: Commission's authority to monitor switching charges
sections.summary: The European Commission can adopt delegated acts to establish a monitoring mechanism for switching charges imposed by data processing service providers. This mechanism ensures compliance with switching deadlines and fee reductions as specified in paragraphs 1 and 2 of the regulation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section grants the Commission authority to monitor switching charges, which impacts DTIA by requiring oversight of financial terms during cross-border data transfers to ensure timely compliance.

requirements.text: Commission may adopt delegated acts to establish monitoring mechanism for switching charges

sections.source_quote: The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 45 to supplement this Regulation by establishing a monitoring mechanism for the Commission to monitor switching charges, imposed by providers of data processing services on the market to ensure that the withdrawal and reduction of switching charges, pursuant to paragraphs 1 and 2 of this Article are to be attained in accordance with the deadlines laid down in those paragraphs.

sections.summary: This section requires trade secrets to be preserved and disclosed only when strictly necessary for agreed purposes. The data holder must identify protected trade secrets and agree on proportionate technical and organisational measures with third parties, such as confidentiality agreements and access protocols. The data holder shall agree with the third party all proportionate technical and organisational measures necessary to preserve the confidentiality of the shared data, such as model contractual terms, confidentiality agreements, strict access protocols, technical standards and the application of codes of conduct

sections.title: Penalty criteria for GDPR violations
sections.summary: Member States must consider EDIB recommendations when determining penalties for GDPR breaches. Penalties are calculated based on infringement severity, mitigation efforts, prior violations, financial impact, and other contextual factors. This applies to national authorities enforcing GDPR compliance.

Member States shall consider the following non-exhaustive criteria for penalty imposition: any previous infringements by the infringing party
Member States shall consider the following non-exhaustive criteria for penalty imposition: the financial benefits gained or losses avoided by the infringing party due to the infringement

sections.source_quote: Member States shall take into account the recommendations of the EDIB and the following non-exhaustive criteria for the imposition of penalties for infringements of this Regulation: (a) the nature, gravity, scale and duration of the infringement; (b) any action taken by the infringing party to mitigate or remedy the damage caused by the infringement; (c) any previous infringements by the infringing party; (d) the financial benefits gained or losses avoided by the infringing party due to the infringement, insofar as such benefits or losses can be reliably established; (e) any other aggravating or mitigating factor applicable to the circumstances of the case; (f) the infringing party's annual turnover in the preceding financial year in the Union.
